heroOverview: Galactosemia (ICD-10: E74.21) is an inherited disorder of galactose metabolism caused by deficient activity of one of three enzymes in the Leloir pathway, resulting in the accumulation of galactose and its toxic metabolites in blood and tissues. Classic galactosemia — caused by deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT) — has a birth prevalence of approximately 1 in 30,000-60,000 in most populations, though it is substantially more common among the Irish Traveller community (around 1 in 480). Untreated, neonates with classic galactosemia rapidly develop life-threatening sepsis, liver failure, and hypoglycaemia within days of milk feeding; newborn screening and immediate dietary galactose restriction prevent acute death. However, even with early and strict dietary treatment, the majority of individuals with classic galactosemia develop long-term complications including intellectual disability, speech apraxia, primary ovarian insufficiency, and neurological abnormalities, highlighting that dietary restriction alone does not prevent all disease manifestations.

keyStats.prevalence: Classic galactosemia (GALT deficiency): approximately 1 in 30,000-60,000 births globally; 1 in 480 births in Irish Traveller community
keyStats.demographics: All ethnicities; autosomal recessive; affects both sexes equally; ovarian insufficiency is sex-specific (females)
keyStats.avgAge: Presents in the neonatal period, typically within days of milk feeding beginning
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 3,000-4,000 new cases annually worldwide; exact figures depend on newborn screening coverage

definition: Galactosemia (ICD-10: E74.21) refers to a group of inherited inborn errors of galactose metabolism caused by enzyme deficiencies in the Leloir pathway, the main route by which galactose is converted to glucose-1-phosphate. Classic galactosemia results from deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase (GALT, encoded by the GALT gene on chromosome 9p13), causing accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate and galactitol in cells throughout the body. Galactokinase deficiency (GALK, ICD-10: E74.29) causes a milder phenotype characterised mainly by cataracts. Galactose epimerase (GALE) deficiency ranges from benign to severe depending on whether it is confined to red blood cells or is generalised. Classic galactosemia follows autosomal recessive inheritance; the most common pathogenic variant in European populations is p.Q188R in the GALT gene, which abolishes enzyme activity. Galactose and galactose-1-phosphate reach toxic concentrations in liver, brain, kidney, and gonads during lactation or any milk-based feeding. In the neonatal period this drives hepatocellular damage, coagulopathy, E. coli sepsis (facilitated by galactose-1-phosphate-mediated inhibition of bactericidal activity), cataracts, and hypoglycaemia. Even on a galactose-restricted diet, endogenous galactose production from UDP-galactose continues, likely accounting for the long-term complications observed despite dietary adherence.

typesClassification.description: Deficiency of galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase; the most common and most severe form. Neonatal crisis with liver failure, jaundice, and E. coli sepsis; long-term: intellectual disability, speech apraxia, neurological abnormalities, and primary ovarian insufficiency in females.
Deficiency of galactokinase; galactitol accumulates and causes cataracts, usually the only significant clinical manifestation. Dietary galactose restriction prevents cataract formation.

Rare; resembles classic galactosemia in presentation and severity. Dietary restriction is required, though GALE deficiency demands careful management as UDP-galactose is also needed for synthesis of essential glycoproteins.

One GALT allele carries a classical mutation and one the Duarte variant N314D; enzyme activity is reduced by about 50-75%. Clinical significance is debated; many affected individuals are identified on newborn screening but have mild or absent symptoms.

primarySymptoms: Neonatal jaundice appearing within days of milk feeding, progressing rapidly to conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia reflecting hepatocellular injury rather than normal physiological jaundice.
Poor feeding, vomiting, and failure to thrive in the first week of life as galactose-1-phosphate accumulates in liver, gut, and brain.
Hepatomegaly and signs of acute liver failure — coagulopathy, elevated transaminases, and later ascites — in untreated neonates within the first 1-2 weeks.
E. coli neonatal sepsis, occurring in a disproportionately high number of untreated galactosemia infants because galactose-1-phosphate impairs neutrophil function.
Hypoglycaemia from hepatic glycogen depletion and impaired gluconeogenesis, contributing to lethargy and seizures in the neonatal period.
Cataracts detectable by slit-lamp examination, caused by galactitol accumulation in the lens; present in all enzyme deficiency subtypes.
Intellectual disability of variable degree — mean IQ approximately 15-20 points below expected — even in patients identified on newborn screening and treated early.
Speech and language disorder, particularly verbal dyspraxia, present in 60-80% of individuals with classic galactosemia and often more disabling than general cognitive impairment.
Neurological signs including tremor, ataxia, and white matter abnormalities on brain MRI, affecting 30-50% of individuals with classic galactosemia.
Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) in 80-90% of females with classic galactosemia, manifesting as delayed puberty, oligo- or amenorrhoea, and infertility despite galactose restriction.
earlyWarningSigns: Prolonged or worsening jaundice in a breastfed or formula-fed neonate beyond the expected physiological peak.
A newborn who is unusually lethargic, refuses feeds, or vomits consistently in the first days of life.
Positive newborn blood spot screen for galactosemia — the most important early signal in screened populations.
A sibling previously diagnosed with galactosemia; in this case, the neonate should be placed on a galactose-free formula immediately at birth pending confirmatory testing.
emergencySigns: Any neonate with jaundice, liver enlargement, and clinical deterioration with suspected or confirmed galactosemia requires immediate dietary galactose elimination — milk should be stopped and replaced with a soy-based or elemental formula — and same-day specialist assessment.

Fever with E. coli bacteraemia in a neonate on milk feeding with abnormal newborn screen is a galactosemia emergency until proven otherwise; galactose restriction and intravenous antibiotics should begin simultaneously.
Bleeding in a neonate — petechiae, prolonged bleeding from venipuncture sites, or ecchymoses — indicating coagulopathy from acute liver failure and requiring immediate clotting factor support.
Seizures or rapidly deepening encephalopathy in a neonate after milk introduction; galactosemia should be excluded urgently.

causes.description: Biallelic mutations in the GALT gene abolish or severely reduce galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase activity, blocking galactose-1-phosphate conversion to UDP-galactose and glucose-1-phosphate. The p.Q188R and p.K285N variants together account for approximately 70% of classic galactosemia alleles in European populations.

The primary toxic intermediate, galactose-1-phosphate, accumulates in hepatocytes, brain neurons, renal tubular cells, and gonadal cells. It inhibits multiple enzymes including phosphoglucomutase and UDP-glucose pyrophosphorylase, disrupting glycogen metabolism, gluconeogenesis, and energy production.

Galactose that cannot enter the Leloir pathway is reduced by aldose reductase to galactitol, which accumulates because no enzyme degrades it. Galactitol in the lens causes osmotic swelling of lens fibres, producing cataracts; galactitol in the brain may contribute to neurological damage.

Even on strict dietary galactose restriction, the body produces galactose endogenously from UDP-galactose in glycoprotein and glycolipid turnover, estimated at 2-4 mg/kg per day. This ongoing internal supply accounts for why complete elimination of exogenous galactose does not prevent long-term complications.

Mutations in GALK (galactokinase) prevent galactose phosphorylation, leading to galactitol accumulation and isolated cataract formation. Generalised GALE deficiency blocks epimerase-mediated interconversion of UDP-galactose and UDP-glucose, producing a phenotype overlapping with GALT deficiency.

All galactosemia subtypes follow autosomal recessive inheritance; both gene copies must be non-functional for classic disease. Carrier parents (heterozygotes) are clinically unaffected but each pregnancy carries a 25% risk of an affected child.

riskFactors.description: Inheriting two non-functional GALT alleles is necessary for classic galactosemia; the p.Q188R variant is most prevalent in European populations and confers null enzyme activity.
The Q188R variant is extremely prevalent in the Irish Traveller community, giving a birth prevalence of approximately 1 in 480 compared with 1 in 30,000-60,000 in the general Irish population.
Related parents are more likely to carry the same recessive GALT variant, raising the probability of biallelic offspring substantially above the population average.

riskFactors.factor: Milk-based feeding in an untreated neonate
riskFactors.description: Both breast milk and standard cow's milk-based formula contain lactose, which is hydrolysed to galactose and glucose in the gut; any milk exposure drives galactose accumulation in an untreated infant. Soy-based formulas are the dietary cornerstone of neonatal management.

Countries without universal newborn blood spot screening for galactosemia cannot identify affected neonates before dietary galactose causes organ damage. Screening allows dietary restriction to begin before symptoms develop.

A previously affected child in the family identifies both parents as carriers; subsequent pregnancies have a 25% risk of galactosemia. Preconception or prenatal testing and formula readiness at birth are recommended.

affectedDemographics: Neonates of all ethnicities; most are identified by newborn blood spot screening in the first days of life
Irish Traveller children, in whom prevalence is approximately 60-125 times the general population rate
Females with classic galactosemia, who have an approximately 80-90% lifetime risk of primary ovarian insufficiency regardless of dietary adherence
School-aged children and adolescents who may have ongoing educational difficulties from verbal dyspraxia and cognitive effects despite early treatment
Adults with classic galactosemia managing lifelong galactose restriction and monitoring for late complications
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis in most screened countries is triggered by an abnormal newborn blood spot screen measuring total galactose (galactose plus galactose-1-phosphate) or GALT enzyme activity in dried blood. A positive screen mandates urgent confirmatory testing on a fresh sample and immediate dietary intervention pending results. Confirmatory testing includes erythrocyte GALT enzyme activity assay (severely reduced or undetectable in classic galactosemia), plasma galactose-1-phosphate concentration (markedly elevated), and GALT gene molecular analysis to identify the causative variant(s). Urine reducing substance testing (positive with galactosuria) has largely been replaced by more specific assays. The Duarte variant produces partial GALT reduction that requires clinical correlation. In an unscreened neonate presenting with jaundice and liver dysfunction, galactosemia should be considered early and urine reducing substances or blood galactose measured urgently; crucially, a normal newborn screen does not exclude galactosemia if the infant has received blood transfusion, which repopulates erythrocytes with donor GALT enzyme. Genetic testing identifies the specific variants, guides prognosis (Q188R homozygotes generally have more severe outcomes than compound heterozygotes), and enables carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis in future pregnancies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Population-level detection before symptoms develop; measures total blood galactose or erythrocyte GALT enzyme activity to identify affected neonates.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small amount of blood is obtained from a heel prick at 24-72 hours of age, blotted onto a filter card, and sent to the laboratory. Results are typically available within 5-7 days; a positive result prompts urgent confirmatory testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms enzyme deficiency and classifies severity; classic galactosemia shows <1% residual activity compared with controls.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous or capillary blood sample is taken; the result may be falsely normal after a blood transfusion because donor red cells contain normal enzyme. Clinical teams consider transfusion history carefully.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the primary toxic metabolite; used for both diagnosis and ongoing dietary monitoring to adjust galactose restriction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fasting blood test; results are available within 1-2 days at a specialist metabolic laboratory. Target on treatment is less than 1-2 mg/dL (0.06-0.1 mmol/L), though exact targets differ by guideline.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative variant(s) to confirm the diagnosis, predict phenotype severity, and enable family cascade testing and prenatal diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A venous blood sample for DNA extraction; results take several weeks. Identifies alleles and predicts whether residual enzyme activity might modulate clinical outcome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A rapid bedside or clinic test demonstrating galactosuria in an acutely unwell neonate before confirmatory tests are available; now less commonly used as a primary diagnostic test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A few drops of fresh urine are applied to a Clinitest tablet; a colour change indicates reducing sugar presence. The test is non-specific — does not distinguish galactose from other reducing sugars — and is always followed by specific quantitative testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and coagulation screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the severity of hepatic involvement at presentation and guides supportive treatment intensity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood test measuring ALT, AST, bilirubin, albumin, prothrombin time, and INR; available within hours in any hospital laboratory.

treatmentOverview: The immediate and lifelong cornerstone of galactosemia management is dietary galactose elimination. In the neonatal crisis, breast milk and all lactose-containing formulas are stopped immediately and replaced with a non-lactose, non-galactose formula — typically a soy-based preparation or a casein hydrolysate formula. This reversal of diet dramatically improves the acute liver disease, jaundice, and sepsis risk within days to weeks. Dietary restriction is then maintained for life, avoiding all dairy products containing lactose (the main dietary source of galactose), as well as foods that contain galactose as free sugar or in galactosides. Galactose restriction prevents cataracts and acute liver injury but, critically, does not prevent all long-term complications — intellectual disability, speech dyspraxia, neurological signs, and primary ovarian insufficiency often develop despite strict adherence, attributed to endogenous galactose production. Monitoring of plasma galactose-1-phosphate every 3-6 months guides dietary adherence but does not reliably correlate with neurocognitive outcomes. Annual developmental and neuropsychological assessment, speech and language therapy, and occupational therapy are integral to long-term management. Female patients should be offered annual gynaecological review from early adolescence, with hormonal replacement therapy started promptly when primary ovarian insufficiency is confirmed, to protect bone density and cardiovascular health. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation are prescribed for all patients on dairy-free diets.

medicalTreatments.name: Dietary galactose elimination (soy-based or hydrolysate formula)
medicalTreatments.description: The primary treatment; all lactose- and galactose-containing foods are excluded from the diet for life. In infancy, breast milk and cow's milk formula are replaced with soy or casein hydrolysate formula. Lifelong avoidance of dairy, lactose-containing medications, and high-galactose foods is maintained.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents acute neonatal liver failure, sepsis, and cataracts in virtually all patients when started before significant organ damage; does not eliminate risk of long-term neurocognitive complications, with intellectual outcomes roughly 15-20 IQ points below population norms even with early treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Hormone replacement therapy for primary ovarian insufficiency
medicalTreatments.description: Oestrogen-progesterone HRT is initiated at the expected age of puberty (or when POI is confirmed) in females with classic galactosemia. It induces secondary sexual characteristics, maintains bone density, and reduces cardiovascular risk. It does not restore fertility.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: HRT normalises bone mineral density trajectory in observational cohorts; the relative reduction in fracture risk mirrors that in post-menopausal HRT literature (approximately 30-40% fewer fractures).

medicalTreatments.name: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Essential for all patients on a dairy-free diet, who cannot meet calcium needs through food alone. Standard supplementation is 1,000-1,200 mg elemental calcium and 600-800 IU vitamin D3 daily, adjusted by serum 25-OH vitamin D levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents dietary deficiency-related bone mineral density loss; target serum 25-OH vitamin D is 50-75 nmol/L; effective in virtually all patients with adherent supplementation.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech and language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Verbal dyspraxia affects 60-80% of children with classic galactosemia and requires specialist speech and language therapy beginning in early childhood. Early, intensive intervention targeting motor planning for speech is more effective than general communication therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Speech intelligibility improves significantly with early specialist input; studies show that intensive therapy before age 5 yields the greatest gains, with approximately 60-70% of children achieving functional communication.

medicalTreatments.name: Neurodevelopmental support and educational intervention
medicalTreatments.description: Structured educational support, including specialised schooling where indicated, occupational therapy for fine motor difficulties, and applied behavioural strategies, addresses the cognitive and adaptive deficits seen in classic galactosemia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: No single pharmacological intervention improves neurocognitive outcomes; educational and therapeutic support improves adaptive function and quality of life in observational outcome studies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Uridine supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational; uridine provides an alternative substrate for UDP-galactose synthesis in tissues, potentially bypassing the GALT block. Preclinical evidence is promising but human trials are limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: N-acetylcysteine antioxidant support
alternativeTreatments.description: Some centres add N-acetylcysteine to management based on evidence that oxidative stress contributes to neurological damage in galactosemia; evidence from human trials remains insufficient for routine recommendation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Galactose-free lactase supplements for trace exposure
alternativeTreatments.description: Lactase enzyme drops are sometimes used when inadvertent small lactose exposures cannot be avoided, though they do not substitute for dietary vigilance.

whySeeSpecialist: Galactosemia requires lifelong co-management by a metabolic physician and specialist metabolic dietitian working within a dedicated inherited metabolic diseases (IMD) service. Neonatal emergencies require same-day referral. Long-term complications spanning neurology, gynaecology, and endocrinology mean that adult patients need coordinated multi-specialty care rather than primary care management alone.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a metabolic medicine specialist (paediatric or adult) with experience in inherited disorders of carbohydrate metabolism, ideally within a national or regional IMD centre. The European Galactosemia Network (GalNet) publishes clinical guidelines and maintains a list of specialist European centres. A metabolic dietitian is essential for dietary education and galactose-1-phosphate monitoring. Females should be under gynaecological review from adolescence for POI monitoring and HRT management.
preventionStrategies: All infants identified as at-risk (positive newborn screen or affected sibling) should be changed to a soy-based formula immediately, before confirmatory results return.
Read food labels carefully for milk, lactose, whey, casein, and dairy ingredients; many processed foods, medications, and supplements contain hidden lactose.
Ask the pharmacist to verify that all prescribed and over-the-counter medications are free from lactose excipients, which are widespread in tablet formulations.
Offer prenatal genetic testing to couples who have previously had an affected child; preimplantation genetic testing is available for families who wish to use it.
Females with classic galactosemia should be referred to gynaecology for baseline anti-Müllerian hormone and FSH measurement at adolescence to establish the trajectory of ovarian function.
Bone mineral density scanning should begin in adolescence for all patients on long-term galactose restriction to detect early osteopenia and guide calcium and vitamin D dosing.
lifestyleModifications: Carry a galactosemia dietary card listing foods that must be avoided, particularly useful when eating at restaurants or travelling.
Use galactose-free calcium-fortified plant milks (oat, almond-free, or rice milk) as dairy substitutes for cooking and drinking.
Educate school staff, coaches, and carers about the dietary restriction so inadvertent exposures can be minimised.
Keep a symptom and dietary diary to correlate any rises in galactose-1-phosphate with specific food exposures.
Plan social events and travel in advance, packing safe foods and identifying galactose-free options at destinations.
Connect with galactosemia patient organisations (UK Galactosaemia Support Group, Parents of Galactosaemic Children USA) for peer support and practical dietary guidance.

recommended: Soy-based infant formula as the exclusive milk substitute from diagnosis; confirm it is explicitly lactose- and galactose-free
Calcium- and vitamin D-fortified soy, oat, or rice milk for older children and adults as a dairy replacement
Fresh fruits and most vegetables, which are low in galactose (except brassica family in large quantities and certain legumes)
Plain meats, poultry, fish, eggs, and legumes as protein sources — all inherently low in galactose
Gluten-containing cereals and bread in the absence of lactose-containing additives
avoid: All dairy milk, cheese, yoghurt, butter, cream, and ice cream — primary sources of dietary lactose and galactose
Breast milk for infants with confirmed classic galactosemia, which contains lactose at approximately 7 g per 100 mL
Foods with milk, whey, casein, or lactalbumin listed in ingredients
Some legumes in large quantities (particularly lentils, broad beans), which contain galactose-containing oligosaccharides
Lactose-containing medications and vitamin supplements — always verify excipients with the pharmacist

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical activity is encouraged for general health, cardiovascular fitness, and bone density maintenance, which is particularly important given the risk of osteopenia from dairy-free diets and primary ovarian insufficiency. There are no galactosemia-specific exercise restrictions. Weight-bearing exercise such as walking, jogging, and resistance training is especially beneficial for bone health. Patients with neurological signs such as tremor or ataxia should work with a physiotherapist to find safe activities.
dailyManagement: Check every food label for dairy-derived ingredients before purchasing; lactose is found in unexpected places including bread, crisps, and medications.
Take calcium and vitamin D supplements daily, at a mealtime if possible.
Females on HRT: take hormone therapy as prescribed without interruption; premature stopping risks bone density loss and vasomotor symptoms.
Attend metabolic clinic reviews every 3-6 months in childhood, and at least annually as an adult, including plasma galactose-1-phosphate measurement.
Maintain routine with speech therapy appointments and educational support services throughout childhood and adolescence.
Inform healthcare providers — including dentists and surgical teams — of the galactosemia diagnosis so that galactose-free medications and dietary considerations are factored into care.
prognosis: With early dietary diagnosis through newborn screening, galactosemia is no longer a cause of neonatal death in screened populations. Cataracts are prevented by galactose elimination when started promptly. The prognosis for long-term neurocognitive, language, neurological, and reproductive outcomes, however, remains guarded. Approximately 50-60% of individuals with classic galactosemia require specialised educational support; most can live independently with appropriate support, but some individuals have significant intellectual disability. Verbal dyspraxia is the most prevalent and often most functionally disabling long-term complication and persists into adulthood in the majority. Primary ovarian insufficiency affects approximately 80-90% of affected females and is irreversible; fertility is significantly impaired, though occasional spontaneous pregnancy has been reported. Life expectancy is not substantially reduced with modern management.
recoveryTimeline: In the neonatal crisis, stopping galactose exposure reverses jaundice and liver dysfunction within 1-4 weeks, and sepsis risk falls rapidly. Cataracts that have formed may partially regress with early galactose restriction in the first weeks of life. Neurocognitive outcomes are established over years of development and do not correspond to a recovery timeline; early and sustained speech therapy from infancy yields the greatest language gains. Primary ovarian insufficiency, once established, does not reverse; hormone replacement therapy is a lifelong management strategy rather than a temporary treatment.
complications: Intellectual disability — mean IQ approximately 15-20 points below expected, affecting roughly 50-60% of individuals to a degree requiring educational support, despite early dietary treatment.
Verbal dyspraxia and speech disorder — affecting 60-80% of individuals; often the most functionally limiting complication in adults.
Primary ovarian insufficiency — 80-90% of females develop POI, leading to infertility, oestrogen deficiency, and accelerated bone and cardiovascular risk.
Neurological complications — tremor, ataxia, and white matter changes on brain MRI occur in 30-50% of individuals, and may progress in severity.

Osteoporosis — from chronic dairy-free diet, oestrogen deficiency in females, and reduced physical activity; bone mineral density scans regularly show Z-scores below −1 in young adults without adequate calcium and vitamin D.
Cataract formation — in untreated or late-diagnosed infants; prevented by early galactose restriction but may be present at diagnosis.

supportResources.name: UK Galactosaemia Support Group
supportResources.description: Patient and family support organisation for galactosaemia in the UK, providing dietary guidance, peer networks, and information about specialist services.

supportResources.name: European Galactosaemia Network (GalNet)
supportResources.description: European clinical network providing evidence-based guidelines, registry data, and links to specialist metabolic centres managing galactosaemia.

supportResources.name: NORD — Galactosemia
supportResources.description: Comprehensive overview of galactosemia including subtypes, genetics, treatment, and links to U.S. support resources.

sources.title: Demirbas D et al. Classic galactosemia. In: GeneReviews [Internet]. NCBI Bookshelf.
Welling L et al. International clinical guideline for the management of classical galactosaemia. J Inherit Metab Dis, 2017.
Coss KP et al. Classic galactosaemia in Ireland: incidence, complications and outcomes. J Inherit Metab Dis, 2013.
Fridovich-Keil JL et al. Ovarian function in girls and women with GALT-deficiency galactosemia. J Inherit Metab Dis, 2011.
ACMG ACT Sheet — Galactosemia: Newborn Screening Follow-up
Bosch AM. Classical galactosaemia revisited. J Inherit Metab Dis, 2006.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Isolated cataracts in neonates can reflect galactokinase deficiency (a galactosemia subtype) or other causes; galactosemia cataracts occur alongside liver disease and systemic findings, while isolated cataract lacks these features.

confusedWithConditions.name: Coeliac disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Coeliac disease causes malabsorption with different dietary triggers (gluten, not galactose) and is confirmed by duodenal biopsy; galactosemia is identified by enzyme assay and genetic testing and presents in the neonatal period.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neonatal sepsis from other causes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: E. coli sepsis in an undiagnosed galactosemia neonate may initially be investigated as isolated neonatal bacterial sepsis; the key distinguishing feature is jaundice, hepatomegaly, and positive reducing substances in urine, prompting galactosemia testing.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Galactitol accumulation in the lens directly causes cataracts in galactosemia; cataract formation is universal without early galactose restriction and is the dominant feature of galactokinase deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Coeliac disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Both require lifelong dietary exclusion managed by a specialist metabolic dietitian; coeliac disease can also cause secondary nutritional deficiencies relevant to bone health management in galactosemia.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic kidney disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Renal tubular dysfunction from galactose-1-phosphate toxicity can contribute to kidney disease in inadequately treated or late-diagnosed galactosemia, and monitoring renal function is part of long-term follow-up.

faqs.question: What is galactosemia?
faqs.answer: Galactosemia is a genetic metabolic disorder where the body cannot properly process galactose, a sugar in milk. The most common form lacks the GALT enzyme, causing toxic sugar build-up that damages the liver, brain, and ovaries. It is identified on newborn blood spot screening.

faqs.question: What causes galactosemia?
faqs.answer: Classic galactosemia is caused by mutations in the GALT gene that eliminate the enzyme galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. Both copies of the gene must be defective (autosomal recessive). This causes galactose-1-phosphate to accumulate and damage multiple organs.

faqs.question: How is galactosemia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Most cases are identified by the newborn blood spot screen measuring GALT enzyme activity or blood galactose. Confirmatory tests include an enzyme activity assay and GALT gene sequencing. Plasma galactose-1-phosphate is measured to assess severity and monitor dietary control.

faqs.question: What can a baby with galactosemia eat?
faqs.answer: Infants with galactosemia must not receive breast milk or standard cow's milk-based formula, as these contain lactose which breaks down to galactose. They are fed a soy-based or casein hydrolysate formula from diagnosis. Dietary restriction continues for life.

faqs.question: Is galactosemia curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for classic galactosemia. Lifelong galactose restriction prevents the acute crisis and cataracts but does not fully prevent intellectual disability, speech difficulties, neurological signs, or ovarian failure. Research into uridine supplementation and gene therapy is ongoing.

faqs.question: Can someone with galactosemia eat dairy products?
faqs.answer: No. All dairy products — milk, cheese, yoghurt, butter, and ice cream — contain lactose, which the body converts to galactose. These must be strictly avoided for life. Calcium-fortified plant milks (soy, oat, or rice-based) are used as alternatives.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of galactosemia?
faqs.answer: Even with early treatment, most individuals with classic galactosemia develop some intellectual disability, verbal dyspraxia (difficulty planning speech movements), and neurological signs. About 80-90% of females develop primary ovarian insufficiency. Life expectancy is near normal with modern management.

faqs.question: Does galactosemia cause infertility in women?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 80-90% of females with classic galactosemia develop primary ovarian insufficiency, meaning the ovaries stop functioning at an early age. This causes infertility in most cases, though occasional spontaneous pregnancy has been reported. Hormone replacement therapy is recommended to protect bone and heart health.

faqs.question: How is galactosemia inherited?
faqs.answer: Galactosemia is autosomal recessive. A child must inherit a defective GALT gene from both parents. Carrier parents each have one working copy and are unaffected. Each pregnancy of two carriers has a 25% chance of producing an affected child.

faqs.question: Does galactosemia cause cataracts?
faqs.answer: Yes. Galactitol, a byproduct of galactose that cannot be processed, accumulates in the lens and causes cataracts. These can develop within weeks of birth in untreated infants. Starting galactose restriction promptly can prevent cataract formation and may cause early lens opacities to regress.

faqs.question: What is primary ovarian insufficiency in galactosemia?
faqs.answer: Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) in galactosemia means the ovaries lose function before age 40, causing low oestrogen, absent or irregular periods, infertility, and risk of osteoporosis. It affects around 80-90% of females despite dietary treatment, and is thought to result from galactose toxicity to ovarian tissue during fetal development.

faqs.question: Are all galactosemia patients the same severity?
faqs.answer: No. Classic galactosemia (GALT null mutations like Q188R) is most severe. The Duarte variant has reduced enzyme activity and usually milder outcomes. Galactokinase deficiency causes mainly cataracts without liver disease. Galactose epimerase deficiency varies from very mild to severe depending on whether it is confined to red blood cells or systemic.

faqs.question: What is verbal dyspraxia in galactosemia?
faqs.answer: Verbal dyspraxia is a disorder affecting motor planning for speech, making it hard to consistently produce words and sounds correctly despite normal muscle strength. It affects 60-80% of children with classic galactosemia and often improves significantly with intensive early speech and language therapy.

faqs.question: What supplements do galactosemia patients need?
faqs.answer: Because dairy foods are excluded, calcium (1,000-1,200 mg daily) and vitamin D supplements are essential for all patients to protect bone density. Females with ovarian insufficiency also need hormone replacement therapy. Levels are monitored at regular metabolic clinic visits.

faqs.question: How is galactosemia monitored long-term?
faqs.answer: Plasma galactose-1-phosphate is measured every 3-6 months to assess dietary adherence. Annual assessments include neuropsychological testing, speech review, bone density scanning in teenagers and adults, and gynaecological review for females. Liver function and kidney function are checked periodically.

faqs.question: Can galactosemia be diagnosed before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prenatal diagnosis is possible by amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling to test the fetal DNA for GALT mutations when both parents are known carriers. Preimplantation genetic testing with IVF is also available, allowing only unaffected embryos to be transferred.

faqs.question: Does galactose restriction prevent all complications?
faqs.answer: No. Galactose restriction prevents neonatal liver failure, sepsis, and cataracts effectively. However, it does not prevent intellectual disability, speech dyspraxia, neurological abnormalities, or primary ovarian insufficiency in most patients, because the body continues producing galactose internally from other metabolic pathways.

faqs.question: What is the difference between galactosemia and lactose intolerance?
faqs.answer: Lactose intolerance is a common digestive condition where the gut lacks lactase enzyme, causing bloating and diarrhoea after dairy but no organ damage. Galactosemia is a rare metabolic disease where a liver enzyme fails, causing galactose to damage the liver, brain, and ovaries — it is life-threatening in infancy without treatment.

faqs.question: Should siblings of a galactosemia baby be tested?
faqs.answer: Yes. Siblings are at 25% risk of also having galactosemia. Any unborn sibling should be offered prenatal testing. Older siblings who were not screened or were screened before the diagnosis was confirmed should have enzyme activity and genetic testing promptly.

faqs.question: What support is available for galactosemia families?
faqs.answer: The UK Galactosaemia Support Group (galactosaemia.org) and the European GalNet network provide dietary guidance, peer support, and specialist referral information. In the U.S., the Parents of Galactosemic Children organisation offers educational resources and family networking for those managing the condition.

metaDescription: Galactosemia is a genetic enzyme deficiency causing toxic galactose build-up in newborns. Learn symptoms, newborn screening, diet, and long-term complications.
